title: The coffee that is already paid for
summary: In a Naples espresso bar, a newcomer learns the local custom of paying for a coffee that someone else will drink.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Buongiorno. One espresso, please. How much is it?

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: One euro. Are you visiting Naples?

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Yes. I am here for two days. I came from Milan.

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: Welcome. Listen, would you like to pay for two coffees?

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: Two? But I only want one.

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: I know. The second one is for somebody else. It is a small Naples tradition. We call it 'caffè sospeso.' Coffee in waiting.

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: I do not know yet. Later today, someone will come in. Maybe an old man with empty pockets. Maybe a man who lost his job. He will ask, 'Is there a sospeso?' I will say yes. I will make him a coffee. He will not pay. You already paid this morning.

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: No. You will be gone. That is the point. He will not thank you. He will not know your face. You will not know his.

body.speaker: Sofia
body.text: How long has the bar done this?

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: My grandfather opened the bar in 1948. He started the sospeso in the first year. Naples was poor after the war. People who could afford a coffee paid for two. People who could not have one still drank one. Seventy-seven years now.

body.speaker: Vincenzo
body.text: Thank you. Drink yours hot. I will write the second on the small blackboard. One sospeso, waiting.

annotations.meaningVi: cà phê espresso (cà phê đậm đặc kiểu Ý)
cà phê 'treo' (cà phê trả trước cho người khác)
túi rỗng / hết tiền (cụm hình ảnh)
cám ơn (ở đây là 'cảm ơn bạn')
đủ tiền để mua / lo được
bảng đen (ở đây: bảng nhỏ trong quán)

grammarNotes.title: Would you like…? (polite offer / invitation)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'Would you like + danh từ / to + V' dùng để mời lịch sự. Trong bài: 'Would you like to pay for two coffees?' (đề nghị lịch sự). Trả lời lịch sự: 'Yes, please.' hoặc 'No, thank you.' Đừng dùng 'Do you want' khi muốn lịch sự — câu đó hơi thẳng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The structure 'Would you like + noun / to + V' is a polite offer or invitation. In the dialogue: 'Would you like to pay for two coffees?' A polite reply uses 'Yes, please.' or 'No, thank you.' 'Do you want…?' is more direct and less polite.

translationVi:
Sofia: Chào anh. Cho tôi một espresso. Bao nhiêu vậy?
Vincenzo: Một euro. Chị đến Naples chơi à?
Sofia: Vâng. Tôi ở đây hai ngày. Tôi từ Milan tới.
Vincenzo: Chào mừng. Chị có muốn trả tiền cho hai ly cà phê không?
Sofia: Hai ly? Nhưng tôi chỉ muốn một.
Vincenzo: Tôi biết. Ly thứ hai là cho người khác. Đó là một phong tục nhỏ của Naples. Bọn tôi gọi là 'caffè sospeso' — cà phê đang chờ.
Sofia: Cho ai?
Vincenzo: Tôi chưa biết. Lát nữa sẽ có người đến. Có thể là ông cụ túi rỗng. Có thể là người vừa mất việc. Họ sẽ hỏi: 'Có sospeso không?' Tôi sẽ nói có. Tôi sẽ pha cho họ một ly. Họ không trả tiền. Sáng nay chị đã trả rồi.
Sofia: Tôi sẽ thấy người đó không?
Vincenzo: Không. Lúc đó chị đi rồi. Cái hay là vậy. Họ không cảm ơn chị. Họ không biết mặt chị. Chị cũng không biết mặt họ.
Sofia: Quán này làm vậy bao lâu rồi?
Vincenzo: Ông tôi mở quán năm 1948. Ông bắt đầu sospeso ngay năm đầu. Sau chiến tranh, Naples nghèo. Ai có tiền cà phê thì trả cho hai. Ai không có vẫn được uống một. Bảy mươi bảy năm rồi.
Sofia: Vậy thì, cho tôi hai ly.
Vincenzo: Cảm ơn. Chị uống lúc còn nóng nhé. Tôi sẽ ghi ly thứ hai lên bảng đen nhỏ. Một sospeso, đang chờ.

options: In an espresso bar in Naples
questions.explanation: Sofia greets and asks for an espresso; the bartender is in Naples.

options: A coffee paid by one person and drunk by another
questions.explanation: Vincenzo explains: 'The second one is for somebody else.'

options: An old man with empty pockets, or someone who lost his job
questions.explanation: 'Maybe an old man with empty pockets. Maybe a man who lost his job.'

questions.prompt: Why does Vincenzo say 'that is the point'?
options: Because the gift works exactly because nobody knows the giver
questions.explanation: 'He will not thank you. He will not know your face.'

questions.prompt: When did Vincenzo's grandfather open the bar?

options: Because Naples was poor after the war
Because the city told him to

questions.prompt: What does Vincenzo do after Sofia agrees?
options: He writes the second coffee on a small blackboard
questions.explanation: 'I will write the second on the small blackboard.'

options: A small anonymous gift can become a city's quiet, lasting custom.
Coffee in Naples is too expensive.
questions.explanation: The seventy-seven-year practice is anonymous and small.

cloze.template:
Sofia: Buongiorno. One {{b1}}, please. How much is it?
Vincenzo: One euro. Are you visiting Naples?
Sofia: Yes. I am here for two days. I came from {{b2}}.
Vincenzo: Welcome. Listen, would you like to pay for two coffees?
Sofia: Two? But I only want one.
Vincenzo: I know. The second one is for {{b3}} else. It is a small Naples tradition. We call it 'caffè sospeso.' Coffee in {{b4}}.
Sofia: For who?
Vincenzo: I do not know yet. Later today, someone will come in. Maybe an old man with empty {{b5}}. Maybe a man who lost his job. He will ask, 'Is there a sospeso?' I will say yes. I will make him a coffee. He will not pay. You already {{b6}} this morning.
Sofia: Will I see this person?
Vincenzo: No. You will be gone. That is the point. He will not thank you. He will not know your face. You will not know his.
Sofia: How long has the bar done this?
Vincenzo: My grandfather opened the bar in {{b7}}. He started the sospeso in the first year. Naples was poor after the {{b8}}. People who could afford a coffee paid for two. People who could not have one still drank one. {{b9}} years now.
Sofia: Okay. Two coffees, please.
Vincenzo: Thank you. Drink yours hot. I will write the second on the small {{b10}}. One sospeso, waiting.

blanks.explanation: He writes it on the small blackboard.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Vincenzo says the point of the tradition is that the giver and the receiver never meet. What does anonymity add to a gift? Could you imagine giving something today that you know will be received by someone you will never see?
